description: 4th-grade teacher submission. MULTI-DOMAIN: word-level reading difficulty (decoding + fluency, somewhat-below) AND self-regulation / attention concerns (task initiation + sustained attention). The two concerns co-occur around independent seatwork so a reader is tempted to causally link them; the teacher never does (P2 stress). Cleared domains carry a mix of faithful in-domain positives (social, communication, hands-on/math) and bare negatives (writing, cognitive, adaptive, motor). One deliberately unresolved teacher musing about which concern drives the other (ambiguity anchor, TCH-IMP-004). Synthetic student D.T. — not a real case. Re-pinned to teacher-intake@1.3.0: the affirmative Cognitive/Adaptive screeners (TCH-COG-000/TCH-ADP-000) are shown (neither flagged on CORE-008) but left unanswered, so both domains render at tier T1 (P29) — not backfilled to demonstrate T2.

informant.relationship: 4th-grade general education homeroom teacher (all core subjects)

responses.TCH-CORE-002: Fourth-grade general education homeroom — I teach him all core subjects.
responses.TCH-CORE-005: He's in a small-group reading intervention three mornings a week. In class I use a printed start-up checklist taped to his desk and let him take a short movement break to help him get going and stay with his work.
responses.TCH-CORE-007: D. is genuinely kind and well-liked — he's usually the first to help a classmate and stays calm when there's a disagreement. He's strongest with hands-on work: building, measuring, anything he can do with his hands, and he's sharp in class discussions when he can just listen and talk.
responses.TCH-RDG-002: When he hits a word he doesn't know, he'll try the first sound and then guess from context, or he just stops and waits for help. Longer, multi-syllable words are where he gets stuck most.
responses.TCH-RDG-003: His reading is slow and halting — he reads word by word and often trails off before the end of a sentence. On grade-level text he's clearly behind the pace of the rest of his reading group.
responses.TCH-RDG-007: Winter DIBELS put him below benchmark on oral reading fluency. He's in the lowest of my three reading groups, and the interventionist's running records have him around 90% accuracy on grade-level text.
responses.TCH-SR-002: Getting started is the hardest part. He'll sharpen his pencil, hunt for materials, ask an unrelated question — anything but begin. Once he's actually started, he can usually do the work.
responses.TCH-SR-003: He drifts within a few minutes of independent work — looking around the room, fidgeting with things in his desk. In a twenty-minute work block I'm redirecting him five or six times.
responses.TCH-SR-005: He does best when he's up and moving or working with a partner, and first thing in the morning. Long stretches of quiet seatwork are when he struggles most.
responses.TCH-INT-001: For reading, the small-group phonics and fluency intervention three times a week. For focus and getting started, a visual start-up checklist taped to his desk, a short movement break, and preferential seating near me. I also email his mother every couple of weeks.
responses.TCH-INT-002: The reading group has been in place since the start of this year — about five months, three 30-minute sessions a week. The checklist and movement break started around the same time.
responses.TCH-INT-003: The reading interventionist runs the group; the classroom supports are mine.
responses.TCH-INT-005: The interventionist charts weekly fluency probes — the trend is slightly up but still below the grade-level target. I don't have a formal chart for the focus supports, just my own notes.
responses.TCH-IMP-001: Both concerns cost him a lot of instructional time. Independent work that should take fifteen minutes can take him the whole block, and content-area reading is hard for him to get through without one-on-one help. He's starting to say he's 'not smart,' which worries me.
responses.TCH-IMP-002: His grades are below my expectations in reading and in subjects that lean on independent reading; in discussion-based and hands-on work he's right where I'd want him.
responses.TCH-IMP-005: People see him drift or not start and read it as laziness or not caring. He cares — he gets frustrated with himself when he can't keep up. It isn't about effort.
responses.TCH-IMP-003: I'd want him reading grade-level text closely enough to keep pace with his group, starting his own work without the checklist, and sticking with a task long enough to finish it.
responses.TCH-IMP-004: Honestly, I go back and forth on whether the reading or the focus is the bigger issue — some days one seems to feed the other, and I couldn't tell you which comes first.
